summary: The first_proof_2 dataset contains 10 research-level mathematics problems, of which 6 (prob-01 through prob-05 and prob-07) have a resolved proof issue and zero open issues, indicating strong traction. Problems 06, 08, 09, and 10 show no recorded proof issues and appear to be the remaining unaddressed targets. The 'Proof Status' counters (q1–q10, each 100 issues) appear to be uniform aggregate metrics rather than differentiators of activity.
problems: prob-06, prob-08, prob-09, and prob-10 have no resolved or open proof issues recorded, suggesting they are untouched or unattempted.
No problem currently has any open issues, which makes it hard to track in-progress work or partial proofs awaiting review.
The uniform 100-issue count across all q1–q10 status entries is ambiguous and may mask the true distribution of effort per problem.
Resolved problems lack visible verification or peer-review issues, so it is unclear whether resolutions have been independently confirmed.
highlights: Six of ten problems (prob-01–05, prob-07) have a resolved proof, a 60% resolution rate across diverse fields.
Resolved problems span descriptive set theory, piecewise-linear geometry, probability, and ergodic theory, demonstrating broad coverage.
Zero open issues across all problems indicates a clean backlog with no stalled or blocked work.
prob-07 (Quasi-Reduced Words) and prob-05 (Unique Ergodicity) being resolved shows progress in both combinatorial and dynamical-systems directions.

suggested_todos.title: Open and assign attack issues for the four unaddressed problems: prob-06, prob-08, prob-09, prob-10
Initiate independent verification/review issues for the six resolved proofs (prob-01–05, prob-07)
Clarify and document what the uniform 100-issue q1–q10 'Proof Status' metric represents
Prioritize prob-06 first since it is the next sequential gap after a strong cluster of solved problems
Establish a tracking convention for partial-progress (open) issues to improve visibility
